h1Title: Gallstones: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Gallstones are hardened deposits of bile constituents — cholesterol, bile salts, and calcium bilirubinate — that form inside the gallbladder. They affect approximately 10-15% of adults in Western countries, with prevalence reaching 20-30% in adults over 70 (Shaffer 2006, Best Pract Res Clin Gastroenterol). Around 80% of gallstones remain asymptomatic throughout life, but when a stone impedes the cystic duct or common bile duct, it triggers biliary colic, acute cholecystitis, or obstructive jaundice. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy removes the gallbladder with stones and cures symptomatic disease; emergency surgery or endoscopic intervention is required for complicated disease.

keyStats.prevalence: 10-15% of adults in Western countries (PMID 16931061); up to 30% in adults over 70; approximately 20 million Americans have gallstones
keyStats.demographics: Women are twice as likely as men to develop gallstones before age 50; parity and oral contraceptives substantially raise risk; Native Americans and Hispanic populations carry the highest prevalence — up to 75% in Pima tribe women
keyStats.avgAge: Symptomatic gallstones peak in adults aged 40-60; incidence rises linearly with age after 30
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 700 million people worldwide have gallstones; cholecystectomy is one of the most commonly performed abdominal surgeries globally, with over 750,000 performed annually in the USA

definition: Gallstones (ICD-10: K80) are solid concretions that develop within the gallbladder from supersaturated bile. Three main types exist by composition: cholesterol stones (75-80% of cases in Western countries) form when bile becomes oversaturated with cholesterol relative to bile salts and lecithin; black pigment stones form from unconjugated bilirubin in haemolytic disease, cirrhosis, and total parenteral nutrition; and brown pigment stones form from calcium bilirubinate in bile-duct infection. Gallstone pathogenesis requires three factors — supersaturated bile (lithogenic bile), cholesterol crystal nucleation, and impaired gallbladder motility allowing crystalline precipitation. Stones range from a few millimetres to several centimetres and may be solitary or multiple. The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile; when stones remain in the gallbladder without obstructing the cystic duct, they are asymptomatic. Cystic duct obstruction produces biliary colic; sustained obstruction causes acute cholecystitis. Stones passing into the common bile duct cause choledocholithiasis, which can result in obstructive jaundice, cholangitis (Charcot's triad: pain, fever, jaundice), and acute pancreatitis. Gallstone disease is managed by gastroenterologists for evaluation, radiologists for imaging, and surgeons for cholecystectomy.

typesClassification.description: Account for 75-80% of Western cases. Form from bile supersaturated with cholesterol when hepatic secretion of cholesterol exceeds bile salt and phospholipid capacity. Associated with obesity, female sex, pregnancy, rapid weight loss, and statin discontinuation.

Composed of calcium bilirubinate polymer; form in haemolytic anaemias (sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis), cirrhosis, Crohn's disease, and prolonged fasting or TPN. Sterile bile; tend to stay in the gallbladder.

Soft, earthy-brown calcium bilirubinate stones associated with bacterial or parasitic bile-duct infections (Clonorchis sinensis, Ascaris lumbricoides). Predominantly form in the bile ducts; more common in East Asia.

Primary stones form de novo in the bile duct (brown pigment); secondary stones migrate from the gallbladder. Present with jaundice, cholangitis, or pancreatitis. Managed with ERCP stone extraction before or instead of cholecystectomy.

primarySymptoms: Biliary colic: sudden, severe right upper quadrant or epigastric pain that builds over 15-30 minutes, plateaus for 1-4 hours, then resolves completely — triggered by cystic duct obstruction when a stone is impacted and released.
Pain radiating to the right shoulder tip or interscapular area, caused by diaphragmatic irritation or referred visceral pain from the biliary system.
Nausea and vomiting accompanying biliary colic episodes, typically during or immediately after the pain peak.
Onset of pain 30-90 minutes after a fatty meal, as cholecystokinin-stimulated gallbladder contraction forces a stone against the cystic duct.

Fever, rigors, and persistent right upper quadrant pain lasting more than 6 hours — the hallmarks of acute cholecystitis when cystic duct obstruction is sustained and the gallbladder becomes inflamed.
Jaundice (yellow skin and sclera), dark urine, and pale stools from common bile duct stone obstruction blocking bile flow into the duodenum.
Acute severe epigastric pain radiating to the back with elevated amylase/lipase — gallstone-induced acute pancreatitis from a stone temporarily impacting the ampulla of Vater.
Murphy's sign positive on examination: inspiratory arrest when the examiner's fingers press below the right costal margin, a clinical marker of acute cholecystitis.
earlyWarningSigns: Episodic right upper quadrant discomfort after fatty meals that resolves within 30-60 minutes — early biliary colic from transient cystic duct impaction without full inflammation.
Unexplained nausea, belching, or bloating after fatty foods in an adult aged 40-60 — may reflect biliary dyspepsia from minor biliary motility disturbance.
Isolated elevation of alkaline phosphatase or GGT on routine liver function tests without obvious hepatic cause — may indicate intermittent bile duct obstruction from a small stone.
Incidentally found gallstones on abdominal ultrasound in an asymptomatic patient — requires counselling about approximately 2% per year risk of first biliary event.
emergencySigns: Fever above 38°C with right upper quadrant pain persisting beyond 6 hours — acute cholecystitis requires urgent hospital admission and IV antibiotics; delay increases perforation risk.

Charcot's triad: fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain together — acute cholangitis, a biliary emergency with 10-30% mortality if untreated. Immediate hospitalization and biliary drainage by ERCP or PTC.
Reynold's pentad: Charcot's triad plus hypotension and confusion — suppurative cholangitis with septicaemia, requiring urgent ICU admission, IV antibiotics, and emergency biliary drainage.
Severe epigastric pain with rapidly rising amylase and lipase — gallstone pancreatitis, which ranges from mild self-limiting to life-threatening severe necrotizing pancreatitis.

causes.description: The liver secretes excess cholesterol relative to bile salts and phospholipids, producing a bile that nucleates cholesterol crystals. Obesity increases hepatic cholesterol synthesis; estrogen stimulates cholesterol secretion. Rapid weight loss mobilizes cholesterol into bile faster than bile salts can keep pace.

A sluggishly emptying gallbladder retains concentrated bile longer, promoting crystal nucleation and growth. Fasting, TPN, pregnancy, and spinal cord injury all reduce gallbladder motility. Gallbladder sludge — a precursor to stones — is the radiological correlate of stasis.

Chronic haemolysis (sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia) floods bile with unconjugated bilirubin that polymerises into calcium bilirubinate stones. Cirrhosis impairs conjugation. Up to 30-50% of sickle cell patients develop gallstones by early adulthood.

causes.cause: Bile acid malabsorption (ileal disease or resection)
causes.description: The terminal ileum recycles bile acids; Crohn's disease or ileal resection breaks the enterohepatic circulation, depleting bile acid pools and promoting cholesterol supersaturation. Patients with Crohn's have 2-3 times the gallstone prevalence of age-matched controls.

causes.cause: Gut microbiome dysbiosis and intestinal motility changes
causes.description: Altered gut microbiota modifies secondary bile acid composition and promotes gallbladder wall inflammation. Long-term use of broad-spectrum antibiotics, constipation, and reduced small-bowel motility all modify bile acid recycling and gallstone risk in observational studies.

riskFactors.description: Women are twice as likely as men to form gallstones before menopause; estrogen stimulates hepatic cholesterol secretion and reduces bile acid synthesis. Risk equalises after menopause unless hormone replacement therapy is used.

Each 5-unit increase in BMI raises gallstone risk by approximately 50%. Visceral obesity increases hepatic cholesterol secretion. Paradoxically, rapid weight loss also precipitates stones — risk peaks at weight loss above 1.5 kg/week.

Estrogen increases cholesterol secretion; progesterone reduces gallbladder motility. Symptomatic gallstones develop in 5-12% of pregnant women; cholecystectomy during pregnancy, if needed, is safest in the second trimester.
Gallstone prevalence doubles each decade from 30 to 70; bile acid synthesis declines with age while cholesterol secretion remains steady, progressively increasing lithogenicity.

riskFactors.factor: Type 2 diabetes mellitus and insulin resistance
riskFactors.description: Insulin resistance reduces gallbladder contractility and promotes lithogenic bile. Diabetic patients have 2-3 times higher symptomatic gallstone risk than non-diabetics (PMID 12612000).

Sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis, and thalassaemia cause pigment gallstones in 30-50% of patients by age 30; cholecystectomy is often planned electively before gallstone complications arise.

riskFactors.factor: Highly restrictive low-calorie diet or fasting
riskFactors.description: Very low calorie diets (<500 kcal/day) or prolonged fasting reduce bile acid synthesis and gallbladder emptying, increasing stone risk by 6-fold during rapid weight loss periods.

affectedDemographics: Native American and Latin American women have the highest gallstone prevalence worldwide — up to 75% of Pima tribe women and 60% of Mexican-American women by age 60.
Northern and Western European populations: 10-20% prevalence in adults; Scandinavian countries have higher rates than Southern Europe.
East Asian populations: lower cholesterol gallstone prevalence but higher brown pigment stone prevalence related to biliary parasitic infections.
Children: gallstones are uncommon but occur in haemolytic anaemia, obesity, and prolonged TPN; paediatric cholecystectomy rates are rising with obesity prevalence.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with history and physical examination: the classic patient presenting with right upper quadrant biliary colic after fatty food, age 40-60, female, and overweight (the '4 Fs' mnemonic). Right upper quadrant tenderness with a positive Murphy's sign on examination suggests cholecystitis. Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line investigation for suspected gallstones, with sensitivity and specificity above 95% for gallbladder stones 3 mm or larger. Ultrasound demonstrates acoustic shadows behind stones, gallbladder wall thickness in cholecystitis, and biliary dilatation in choledocholithiasis. Liver function tests, full blood count, and serum amylase are performed to assess for cholestasis, infection, and pancreatitis. If ultrasound does not visualize the common bile duct stone but there is clinical or biochemical suspicion of choledocholithiasis (elevated bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, GGT, or dilated duct on ultrasound), MRCP (magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography) is the next step — non-invasive, radiation-free, and 90-95% sensitive for bile duct stones. Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) reaches 97% sensitivity for small duct stones but is invasive. ERCP is reserved for therapeutic stone extraction, not diagnostic purposes alone. CT of the abdomen is useful for suspected complications (perforation, emphysematous cholecystitis, gallstone ileus) but poor for detecting cholesterol stones, which are isodense to bile.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line test; detects gallbladder stones with 95%+ sensitivity, assesses gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, and biliary dilatation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the right upper abdomen and a handheld probe is moved across the skin. Takes 15-20 minutes. Fasting for 4-6 hours maximizes gallbladder distension and stone visibility. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests (bilirubin, ALP, GGT, ALT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for bile duct obstruction (raised bilirubin, ALP, GGT) and hepatocellular injury (raised ALT); guides need for MRCP to assess for choledocholithiasis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within a few hours in most laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive visualization of the bile ducts and pancreatic duct; detects bile duct stones with 90-95% sensitivity; performed when ultrasound shows duct dilatation or LFTs suggest obstruction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in an MRI scanner; no contrast needed. Biliary and pancreatic ducts are visualized as bright fluid-filled structures. Results read by radiologist within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses gallstone pancreatitis; lipase above 3 times the upper limit of normal is diagnostic of acute pancreatitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 2-4 hours. Lipase is more specific than amylase for acute pancreatitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Therapeutic removal of common bile duct stones; sphincterotomy and stone extraction performed at the same sitting; reserved for confirmed choledocholithiasis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under sedation. An endoscope is passed through the mouth to the duodenum; the bile duct opening is cannulated. Post-ERCP pancreatitis occurs in 3-5% of cases. Patient fasts for 6 hours beforehand.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for complications: perforation, abscess, gallstone ileus, emphysematous cholecystitis, and gallbladder carcinoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15 minutes in a CT scanner; intravenous contrast is usually given. Poor at detecting cholesterol stones but excellent for complications and staging.

treatmentOverview: Management depends on whether gallstones are symptomatic, complicated, or incidental. Asymptomatic gallstones in an otherwise healthy adult do not require treatment; the risk of first biliary events is approximately 1-2% per year and prophylactic cholecystectomy is not recommended except in specific high-risk groups (sickle cell disease, porcelain gallbladder, a single large stone above 3 cm in size). Symptomatic gallstone disease (biliary colic) is treated by laparoscopic cholecystectomy — the definitive, minimally invasive removal of the gallbladder. NICE 2014 and SAGES 2010 guidelines recommend laparoscopic cholecystectomy for all symptomatic gallstones. Acute cholecystitis is managed with IV antibiotics (cefazolin or piperacillin-tazobactam) and laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 24-72 hours in fit patients (Tokyo Guidelines 2018 recommendation); early surgery reduces complications versus delayed. Common bile duct stones are treated by ERCP with sphincterotomy and balloon/basket extraction; interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy is performed after ductal clearance. Gallstone pancreatitis: mild pancreatitis recovers conservatively; cholecystectomy is performed before discharge or within 2 weeks to prevent recurrence (45% of patients have a recurrent event within 6 weeks without definitive treatment). Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA, 8-10 mg/kg/day) dissolves small radiolucent cholesterol stones in 6-24 months in patients unfit for surgery; success rate is 50-70% but recurrence occurs in 50% within 5 years of stopping treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA, 8-10 mg/kg/day orally)
medicalTreatments.description: Enriches bile with hydrophilic bile acid to reduce cholesterol saturation, gradually dissolving small (<10 mm) radiolucent cholesterol stones. Requires 6-24 months of therapy. Reserved for patients unfit for surgery or who decline surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete dissolution in 50-70% of patients with ideal stone characteristics; recurrence in 50% at 5 years without ongoing low-dose maintenance (PMID 1727731)

medicalTreatments.name: IV antibiotics for acute cholecystitis (cefazolin or piperacillin-tazobactam)
medicalTreatments.description: Administered on hospital admission to control biliary infection and prevent sepsis. Gram-negative enterobacteriaceae and anaerobes are the primary pathogens in biliary infection. Antibiotic choice follows local resistance patterns.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces bacteraemia and septic complications; Tokyo Guidelines grade I/II cholecystitis has <0.5% mortality with timely surgery and antibiotics

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesia: NSAIDs (diclofenac 75 mg IM or IV)
medicalTreatments.description: NSAIDs are first-line analgesics for biliary colic; they reduce prostaglandin-driven smooth muscle spasm and have been shown to reduce the risk of progression to acute cholecystitis compared with opioids. IV diclofenac or ketorolac provides faster relief.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Time to pain relief 30-60 minutes; NSAIDs superior to antispasmodics (hyoscine) for biliary colic in Cochrane review (PMID 26716642)

medicalTreatments.name: Extended-release antibiotic prophylaxis with UDCA (for post-bariatric surgery patients)
medicalTreatments.description: UDCA 300-600 mg/day for 6 months after bariatric surgery reduces gallstone formation during rapid weight loss by 39-53%. Recommended in NICE and EAES guidelines for patients undergoing gastric bypass.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Gallstone formation reduced from 36% to 2% with UDCA versus placebo in gastric bypass cohort (PMID 8317451)

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the gallbladder via 3-4 small abdominal incisions using laparoscopic technique; standard of care for symptomatic gallstones. Most patients are discharged same day or within 24 hours. Bile duct injury occurs in 0.3-0.5%.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure rate greater than 99% for biliary colic; conversion to open surgery required in 2-5% of cases; bile duct injury in 0.3-0.5%; 30-day mortality under 0.1% in elective cases

surgicalOptions.name: Open cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Traditional open surgery through a right subcostal incision; reserved for cases where laparoscopic approach is unsafe (severe adhesions, Mirizzi syndrome, gallbladder carcinoma, intraoperative complications).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Same cure rate as laparoscopic; hospital stay 3-5 days; wound complications higher than laparoscopic; conversion from laparoscopic to open in approximately 4% of cases

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous cholecystostomy
surgicalOptions.description: Ultrasound or CT-guided drain placed into the gallbladder to decompress acute cholecystitis in patients too unfit for immediate surgery (septic shock, ASA IV-V). Bridges to interval cholecystectomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Clinical improvement in 80-90% within 48-72 hours; used as bridge to interval surgery at 6-8 weeks

surgicalOptions.name: ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic treatment for common bile duct stones before or after cholecystectomy. Sphincterotomy opens the bile duct outlet; stones are extracted with a balloon or basket catheter. Success rate above 90% for duct clearance.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Ductal clearance in over 90% of cases at first or second attempt; post-ERCP pancreatitis 3-5%; perforation under 1%

alternativeTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL)
alternativeTreatments.description: Focused shock waves fragment single radiolucent cholesterol stones below 20 mm in a gallbladder with preserved function; rarely used since laparoscopic cholecystectomy became standard, but an option in specific patients with a solitary stone refusing surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary fat restriction during attacks
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing dietary fat intake diminishes cholecystokinin-driven gallbladder contractions and lowers biliary colic frequency in patients awaiting cholecystectomy. Not a definitive treatment but reduces symptom burden.

whySeeSpecialist: Gastroenterologist referral is indicated for suspected common bile duct stones, gallstone pancreatitis, or obstructive jaundice. Surgical referral is appropriate for all symptomatic gallstones, recurrent biliary colic, or acute cholecystitis. Endoscopist involvement is needed for ERCP in choledocholithiasis.
doctorSelectionGuide: For elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy, choose a general surgeon with a documented volume of at least 50 procedures annually; bile duct injury rate at high-volume centres is 3-4 times lower than low-volume centres. For acute cholangitis, any centre with 24-hour ERCP access and ICU support is appropriate.
hospitalCriteria: Surgical unit with laparoscopic cholecystectomy capability and 24-hour emergency surgery
Endoscopy unit with ERCP capability for bile duct stone management
Radiology with 24-hour ultrasound and MRCP availability
ITU/HDU support for complicated gallstone disease (cholangitis, severe pancreatitis)
keyFacilities: General surgical departments at district general or university hospitals with elective and emergency laparoscopic cholecystectomy programs
Hepatopancreatobiliary (HPB) units for complex choledocholithiasis, Mirizzi syndrome, and suspected gallbladder carcinoma
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy body weight and avoid crash diets — very low calorie diets (less than 500 kcal/day) increase stone risk 6-fold; aim for weight loss of 0.5-1 kg/week maximum.
Take ursodeoxycholic acid (300-600 mg/day) for 6 months following bariatric surgery to prevent rapid-weight-loss-related gallstone formation.
Exercise moderately 30 minutes most days — physically active individuals have 30-40% lower gallstone risk than sedentary counterparts.
Eat a diet rich in fibre, fruits, vegetables, and unsaturated fats; reduce refined carbohydrates and saturated fats that promote lithogenic bile.
Control blood glucose and insulin resistance with diet and exercise — type 2 diabetes doubles symptomatic gallstone risk.
lifestyleModifications: Eat regular meals with moderate fat content to promote regular gallbladder emptying and prevent bile stasis — skipping meals worsens stasis.
Reduce total calorie intake to achieve gradual sustained weight loss rather than rapid cycles of weight gain and loss.
Limit saturated fat (fatty meats, dairy, processed foods) while including moderate amounts of unsaturated fat to stimulate healthy gallbladder contraction.
Increase dietary fibre (oats, legumes, vegetables) to reduce bile cholesterol saturation by binding bile acids in the gut.
Report recurrence of biliary pain promptly — second attacks have a 50-60% chance of progressing to cholecystitis if cholecystectomy is deferred.

recommended: High-fibre foods (oats, lentils, vegetables, whole grains) to reduce cholesterol saturation of bile
Olive oil and omega-3 fatty acids — moderate fat stimulates gallbladder emptying; olive oil reduces cholesterol crystal formation
Fruits and vegetables rich in vitamin C — ascorbic acid is an essential cofactor for bile acid synthesis; deficiency correlates with gallstone risk
Coffee (3-4 cups/day) — regular coffee consumption is associated with 20-25% lower gallstone risk in meta-analyses, via bile acid and gallbladder motility effects
avoid: High-fat, fried, and fatty foods that trigger gallbladder contraction and biliary colic attacks
Refined carbohydrates (white bread, sugar, pastries) that raise triglycerides and promote insulin resistance linked to gallstone formation
Very low calorie diets and prolonged fasting that reduce bile acid secretion and promote stasis
Excessive alcohol, which raises triglycerides though moderate alcohol consumption is paradoxically associated with lower gallstone risk in some studies

exerciseGuidelines: At least 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walking, cycling, swimming) reduces gallstone risk by 30-40% in prospective cohort studies. Regular exercise improves bile composition, reduces insulin resistance, and promotes gallbladder motility. After laparoscopic cholecystectomy, light walking is encouraged from day 1; return to full activity in 1-2 weeks.
dailyManagement: Eat three moderate-sized meals at regular intervals — consistent meal timing prevents prolonged gallbladder stasis.
Carry prescribed analgesia (diclofenac suppository or oral) for biliary colic attacks while awaiting cholecystectomy.
Attend cholecystectomy promptly after a first symptomatic episode — deferral beyond 6 weeks increases risk of complications.
After cholecystectomy, maintain a low-fat diet for 4-6 weeks to allow intestinal adaptation to continuous bile flow, reducing post-cholecystectomy diarrhoea.
Report fever, jaundice, or severe persistent pain after cholecystectomy — rare but requires urgent assessment for retained duct stones.
prognosis: Prognosis after laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones is excellent; greater than 90% of patients are cured of biliary colic, with less than 5% experiencing residual postcholecystectomy syndrome from bile acid malabsorption or functional dyspepsia. Operative mortality is under 0.1% in elective cases. Asymptomatic gallstones carry approximately 2% per year risk of developing symptoms; over 20 years, roughly 20% of asymptomatic patients develop symptoms. Complicated disease (cholecystitis, cholangitis, pancreatitis) carries progressively higher morbidity: acute cholecystitis mortality is below 1% with timely surgery, acute cholangitis mortality is 5-10%, and severe gallstone pancreatitis carries 15-20% mortality without intensive support. Post-cholecystectomy, a small subset (5-10%) develop bile acid diarrhoea from continuous bile flow into the colon, managed effectively with cholestyramine or colestipol.
recoveryTimeline: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy: same-day or 23-hour stay; return to desk work 3-5 days; return to physical labour 2-3 weeks. ERCP for bile duct stones: same-day procedure; 1-2 days observation if pancreatitis risk is elevated. Acute cholecystitis: 2-5 days hospital stay with early surgery; return to normal activity 2-3 weeks post-discharge.
complications: Acute cholecystitis — untreated biliary colic progresses to cholecystitis in 10-15% of episodes; 48 hours of sustained cystic duct obstruction leads to gangrene or perforation.
Acute cholangitis from bile duct stone obstruction — life-threatening sepsis requiring emergency biliary drainage by ERCP; mortality 10-30% if drainage is delayed beyond 24 hours.

Gallstone pancreatitis — a stone transiently obstructing the ampulla of Vater triggers pancreatitis in 5-8% of patients with choledocholithiasis; severity ranges from mild to fatal necrotizing pancreatitis.
Gallbladder carcinoma — rare but associated with large (>3 cm) stones, porcelain gallbladder, and chronic gallstone disease; 5-year survival below 10% when detected at late stage.
Mirizzi syndrome — external compression of the common bile duct by a large impacted cystic duct stone causing cholestasis and mimicking cholangiocarcinoma on imaging.
Gallstone ileus — rare mechanical small bowel obstruction from a large stone eroding through a cholecystoduodenal fistula; presents with intermittent bowel obstruction requiring surgery.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on gallstones, cholecystitis, and treatment options from the leading US gastroenterology professional society.

supportResources.name: NHS UK — Gallstones
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS patient guide covering symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, and what to expect from laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

supportResources.name: British Society of Gastroenterology — Gallstones Guidance
supportResources.description: BSG clinical guidance on acute cholangitis and gallstone pancreatitis for patients and clinicians.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcer pain is typically epigastric, burning, related to food or relieved by antacids, and not colicky in pattern. Gallstone biliary colic is right upper quadrant, constant during the attack (not waxing and waning), and resolves completely. Upper endoscopy confirms peptic ulcer; ultrasound confirms gallstones.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD causes retrosternal heartburn, regurgitation, and nocturnal symptoms, worsened by lying flat. Gallstones produce right upper quadrant pain and nausea after fatty meals without acid reflux. Upper endoscopy and abdominal ultrasound distinguish the two conditions; both may coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS causes variable abdominal pain with altered bowel habit (constipation, diarrhea, or alternating) relieved by defaecation, without discrete 2-4 hour pain episodes. Gallstone biliary colic produces acute severe right upper quadrant pain lasting 1-4 hours with complete resolution. Ultrasound and normal LFTs in IBS confirm the distinction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatitis B causes diffuse liver inflammation with elevated transaminases (ALT, AST), fatigue, and jaundice; right upper quadrant tenderness is diffuse rather than focal. Gallstones cause focal Murphy's sign and colic; transaminases are normal or minimally raised unless duct obstruction is present. Serology (HBsAg) and ultrasound distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: GERD
relatedConditions.relevance: GERD and biliary disease commonly coexist and both present with epigastric symptoms; both enter the differential for upper abdominal pain after meals.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Postcholecystectomy bile acid diarrhoea resembles IBS-D; both conditions may coexist and both are managed with dietary modification and cholestyramine.

relatedConditions.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions cause epigastric pain and are among the commonest causes of upper abdominal pain requiring investigation in adults.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of gallstones?
faqs.answer: Most gallstones cause no symptoms. When symptoms occur, the typical presentation is sudden severe pain in the right upper abdomen or epigastrium after a fatty meal, lasting 1-4 hours then resolving completely. Nausea, vomiting, and right shoulder pain often accompany the attack. Fever with persistent pain suggests cholecystitis.

faqs.question: Where is gallstone pain located?
faqs.answer: Gallstone biliary colic typically produces pain in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen, just below the right rib margin, or in the epigastrium (pit of the stomach). Pain often radiates to the right shoulder tip or between the shoulder blades. It is triggered by fatty meals and builds over 15-30 minutes.

faqs.question: How are gallstones diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line test, detecting gallbladder stones with over 95% accuracy. Liver function tests identify bile duct obstruction. MRCP is used when ultrasound suggests a common bile duct stone. CT is reserved for complications. ERCP is therapeutic, not diagnostic.

faqs.question: Do gallstones always need surgery?
faqs.answer: No. About 80% of gallstones are asymptomatic and do not require treatment. Surgery (laparoscopic cholecystectomy) is recommended after the first symptomatic biliary colic episode because the risk of a second attack with complications is 50-60% within one year of the first event without treatment.

faqs.question: What is a gallbladder attack?
faqs.answer: A gallbladder attack (biliary colic) occurs when a gallstone blocks the cystic duct, causing severe right upper quadrant pain that builds over 30 minutes, plateaus for 1-4 hours, then subsides. It is not a true colic (the pain does not wax and wane). Fatty food is the commonest trigger.

faqs.question: What happens if gallstones are left untreated?
faqs.answer: Asymptomatic stones have a low risk — about 2% per year of developing symptoms. Once symptomatic, the risk of complications (cholecystitis, cholangitis, pancreatitis) rises substantially. Acute cholangitis from a bile duct stone can be fatal without urgent treatment. Elective cholecystectomy after the first attack prevents these complications.

faqs.question: How long does laparoscopic cholecystectomy take?
faqs.answer: The operation typically takes 45-75 minutes under general anaesthesia. Most patients are discharged the same day or within 24 hours. Return to sedentary work is typically 3-5 days; return to manual labour 2-3 weeks. Bile duct injury is a rare but serious complication occurring in 0.3-0.5% of cases.

faqs.question: Can you live without a gallbladder?
faqs.answer: Yes. The gallbladder is a storage reservoir for bile, not essential to digestion. After cholecystectomy, bile flows continuously from the liver into the duodenum. About 5-10% of patients have temporary loose stools from bile acid load, usually resolving within weeks. Long-term quality of life is normal in over 90% of patients.

faqs.question: What foods should you avoid with gallstones?
faqs.answer: Fatty, fried, and greasy foods trigger gallbladder contractions that cause biliary colic. Refined carbohydrates (white bread, sugar) and large meals also increase risk. A low-fat diet with regular meals reduces attack frequency while awaiting cholecystectomy. No specific food permanently prevents gallstone formation.

faqs.question: Are gallstones dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most gallstones are not dangerous. Complications are serious but preventable: acute cholecystitis, acute cholangitis (bile duct infection), and gallstone pancreatitis each carry meaningful morbidity. Acute cholangitis has 5-10% mortality without urgent biliary drainage. Elective cholecystectomy after the first attack prevents most complications.

faqs.question: Can gallstones dissolve naturally?
faqs.answer: Small cholesterol gallstones very occasionally dissolve on their own, but this is uncommon. Ursodeoxycholic acid medication can dissolve small (under 10 mm) radiolucent cholesterol stones in 6-24 months in 50-70% of cases but recurrence is high. Most symptomatic gallstones require cholecystectomy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between gallstones and kidney stones?
faqs.answer: Gallstones form in the gallbladder from bile constituents (cholesterol, bilirubin) and cause right upper quadrant pain after fatty meals. Kidney stones form in the kidneys from urinary minerals and cause severe flank pain radiating to the groin with blood in the urine. They are detected by different imaging methods and treated differently.

faqs.question: Can pregnancy cause gallstones?
faqs.answer: Yes. Estrogen during pregnancy increases cholesterol secretion into bile; progesterone reduces gallbladder motility. Symptomatic gallstones develop in 5-12% of pregnant women. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy, if needed, is safest in the second trimester and is routinely performed without harm to the fetus.

faqs.question: What is acute cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Acute cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder from sustained cystic duct obstruction by a gallstone. It causes fever, persistent right upper quadrant pain lasting more than 6 hours, and a positive Murphy's sign on examination. Treatment is IV antibiotics and laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 24-72 hours of diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can children get gallstones?
faqs.answer: Yes, though uncommon. Pediatric gallstones are associated with haemolytic anaemia (sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis), obesity, prolonged TPN, and Crohn's disease. Sickle cell patients develop pigment gallstones in 30-50% of cases by early adulthood; elective cholecystectomy is often planned proactively.

symptomKeywords: severe right upper abdomen pain after eating
metaDescription: Gallstones: deposits in the gallbladder causing right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and jaundice. Ultrasound diagnosis and cholecystectomy options explained.
